title: Understanding Linux File Permissions
description: Learn how Linux file permissions work — read, write, execute, chmod, chown, and why you get 'Permission denied'.

steps.title: Why 'Permission denied' happens
steps.content: Linux controls who can read, write, and run every file. When you see "Permission denied", it means your user account doesn't have the right permission for that action. This is a security feature — it keeps you from accidentally breaking your system and stops malware from modifying system files.

steps.title: Read permission letters
steps.content:
List a file's permissions with `ls -l`. You'll see something like `-rwxr-xr--`. Here's how to read it:

- Position 1: file type (`-` = file, `d` = directory)
- Positions 2-4: **owner** permissions (rwx)
- Positions 5-7: **group** permissions (r-x)
- Positions 8-10: **others** permissions (r--)

Each position is: `r` = read, `w` = write, `x` = execute, `-` = no permission.
steps.code:
ls -l myfile.txt
# Output: -rw-r--r-- 1 gabriel users 1024 Mar 5 10:00 myfile.txt
# Owner: read+write | Group: read | Others: read

steps.title: Change permissions with chmod
steps.content: Use `chmod` to add or remove permissions. The `+` adds a permission, `-` removes it. Use `u` (owner), `g` (group), `o` (others), or `a` (all).
steps.code:
# Make a file executable:
chmod +x script.sh

# Remove write permission for others:
chmod o-w myfile.txt

# Give owner full access, read-only for everyone else:
chmod u=rwx,go=r myfile.txt

steps.title: Numeric (octal) permissions
steps.content:
You'll often see permissions written as numbers like `755` or `644`. Each digit is a sum: **r=4**, **w=2**, **x=1**.

- `7` = rwx (4+2+1)
- `6` = rw- (4+2)
- `5` = r-x (4+1)
- `4` = r-- (4)

Common combos: `755` = owner full, others read+execute. `644` = owner read+write, others read-only.
steps.code:
# Set 755 permissions:
chmod 755 script.sh

# Set 644 permissions:
chmod 644 document.txt

steps.title: Change file ownership with chown
steps.content: Each file has an owner and a group. Use `chown` to change them. You need `sudo` for this.
steps.code:
# Change owner:
sudo chown gabriel myfile.txt

# Change owner and group:
sudo chown gabriel:users myfile.txt

# Change ownership of a folder and everything inside:
sudo chown -R gabriel:users /path/to/folder/

steps.title: When to use sudo vs changing permissions
steps.content:
- Use **sudo** for one-time system tasks (installing software, editing system config)
- Use **chmod/chown** when you need to permanently fix who can access a file
- **Never** run everything as root or `chmod 777` everything — that defeats the entire security model
steps.warning: Avoid chmod 777 — it gives everyone full access. If you think you need 777, you probably need to change the file owner instead.

seo.title: Linux File Permissions Explained — chmod, chown, rwx
seo.description: Understand Linux file permissions — what rwx means, how to use chmod and chown, numeric permissions like 755, and fixing 'Permission denied' errors.
